review_text: Moving and powerful, start to finish. Cried during the end

Alongside 'Children of Men' this movie 'American History X' seems to explain what is happening in America today. AHX has powerful imagery and strong emotions but at times doesn't ring entirely true but that may be a narrative device to bring us closer to a predetermined conclusion. For the sake of artistry and storytelling, realism is sacrificed, but there may be a lesson in the story that is being told which some may feel overrides other considerations. The ending, however, reeks of realism but the movie goes out on a point of idealism and this contradiction might explain why America is where it is today on the issue of race relations.
If didactic and overwrought at times, it's also powerful and persuasive
I need to watch it again, but from what I remember, it was brutal and heavy. An important movie to see.

If I could give this film more stars, I would. American History X masterfully presents real-world social issues through compelling fictional characters and an unflinching narrative. The performances are extraordinary, with each actor bringing depth, authenticity, and emotional resonance to their roles. The writing is powerful, the direction is exceptional, and the film’s message is both timeless and deeply impactful. It’s the kind of movie that stays with you long after the credits roll. I only wish I could experience it again for the first time. An unequivocal five stars.
This whole film just hurts. A masterpiece
American History X is brutal, furious, and painfully relevant — a film that stares straight into hate and refuses to blink.

American History X is one of the most emotionally intense and thought-provoking films ever made. It tackles difficult themes with honesty and depth, forcing viewers to confront the realities of hatred, redemption, and change. Edward Norton delivers a career-defining performance — raw, complex, and unforgettable. The film doesn’t shy away from showing darkness, but it also doesn’t leave you without hope. It’s a brutally honest story that manages to be both disturbing and deeply human, and it handles its subject matter with care, intelligence, and purpose. This is not an easy film to watch — but it’s an essential one. American History X is the kind of movie that stays with you long after it ends, challenging your perspective and leaving you changed.

Although it could have delved slightly deeper into some of the character changes and plot parallels, American History X is a grim, uncompromising and thought-provoking crime drama whose important message is as strong and moving as the amazing performances from Edward Norton and Edward Furlong.

Ed Norton's Derek Vinyard is one of the all-time best performances in film; he is intelligent, quick, scary and intimidating all at the same time (i.e. he doesn't play a one-dimensional character). I also love the arc of the character and how he genuinely goes through something transformative; it is a massively thought-provoking film and it ends shockingly and sticks with you well after it has ended.

Ce n'est pas le genre de films dont j'ai l'habitude de parler ici, mais bon, il y a un début à tout. Les scènes sont graphiques et même si l'ont ne voit pas tout, on comprend et absorbe le message. C'est un film qui parle de la réalité, cette histoire elle existe et elle va continuer d'exister. Le racisme est une chose qui a traversé les époques et contrairement à ce que tout le monde pense, ça ne s'améliore pas, ça empire. Plusieurs disent que tout va bien, mais le problème grossit dans l'ombre. Ce film est une preuve que l'on peut passer outre cette idéologie d'haine raciale et d'avancer vers quelque chose qui nous uni tous. La peur mène à la colère, la colère mène à la haine, la haine mène à la souffrance. Cette phrase a été dite par un bonhomme vert. Si un homme ne peut pas accepter une nuance, comment peut-il accepter une différence. Amour et paix.
One of the best movie's of all time.

A 90's classic with a career defining performance from Norton. It's a non linear near epic about Derek (Norton), who is a neo-Nazi. While the movie doesn't deal as head on with its own ethos as I may have liked, the messaging is still very strong and effective. It's a heartbreaking drama on one hand and a cautionary tale on the other that can come off very "of the era" in some ways, but still as timely as ever. It's a difficult watch at times, but I think there isn't anything exactly like it either - which makes this a crucial piece of cinema in one way or another.

Unfortunately this is as relevant as when it was made of not even more so. The subjects it tackles are so layered, nuance, and relatable and is done in a raw, unforgiving, and thought provoking way. This is 2 things. An All Time Classic and also something for most is only something you need to see once. The only minor issue I have is it can beat you over the head sometimes with the white supremacy monologues Derek goes on in flashbacks at times but it is realistic how groups like this actually are so I can appreciate that. Edward Norton is a HUGE reason why this works so well. Everything is pretty much flawless save for the music which is well done but can be a bit over the top sometimes and out of place. I wish it was a bit more distinct for such a surreal subject and presentation. Masterful editing and use of black and white in flashbacks that never feel out of place. And its use of extreme violence is never done for shock value but to drive home its message of the consequences of engaging in this life and it's done sparingly. Lots of others I've seen go too over the top and are excessive with that. This is something that someday hopefully will be seen as a relic of the past but still will never diminish its artistic merits as well. Think after watching this.
Excellent movie, but hard to watch at times. Very powerful movie.
